text: This operation begins with
The surgeon sweeps the small intestine to the right side of the abdomen, repositioning it within the surgical field.
The surgeon elevates the cecum to reveal the tenting of the right colic artery, highlighting its position and tension within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon dissects around the right colic artery, preparing to use a vascular stapler to transect the artery at its base.
The surgeon performs a high ligation of the right colic artery, a critical step in the cancer operation.
The surgeon uses the side of the transected right colic artery to gain entry and dissect the mesentery away from the retroperitoneum.
The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, progressively elevating the colon to facilitate the separation of the mesentery from the retroperitoneum.
The surgeon dissects the mesentery towards the hepatic flexure, carefully identifying and protecting the duodenum during the procedure.
The surgeon dissects the transverse colon, proximal to the middle colic artery, freeing it from the surrounding omentum.
The surgeon rolls the transverse colon and hepatic flexure in a caudal direction, facilitating the dissection process by progressively elevating these structures.
A tunnel is created under the transverse colon, preparing for a stapled transection at the distal end of the specimen.
The surgeon uses two staple loads to completely transect the transverse colon at the distal end of the specimen, following the creation of a tunnel under the transverse colon.
The surgeon dissects the hepatic flexure of the colon, freeing it from surrounding tissues, and demonstrates the mobilization of the right hemicolon.
The surgeon gradually connects the dissection planes from the hepatic flexure to the initial medial to lateral dissection, ensuring continuity between the dissected areas.
The surgeon takes down the lateral attachments, freeing the lateral portion of the ascending colon, which is visible as the dissection progresses.
The surgeon transects the proximal small intestine approximately two centimeters proximal to the ileocecal valve, completing the separation of this section from the rest of the intestinal tract.
The surgeon arranges and elevates the right hemicolon from the surgical field, revealing the remaining mesentery attachments that are still connected.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to transect the mesentery, fully freeing the entire specimen from its attachments.
The surgeon positions the terminal ileum near the transverse colon in an isoparastaltic orientation, aligning the bowel segments for anastomosis.
Endocyanin green dye is injected, and the fluorescence lens of the da Vinci robot is used to visualize the vascular perfusion of the anastomotic limbs, highlighting the blood flow in these areas.
The surgeon places stay sutures at the proximal and distal ends of the planned anastomosis, facilitating manipulation of the intestinal segments.
The surgeon creates enterotomies in both the colon and the small intestine, followed by performing a side-by-side stapled anastomosis to connect the two sections.
The surgeon visually inspects the anastomosis, confirming the absence of intraluminal bleeding along the staple line.
The surgeon oversews the common channel enterotomy in two layers, ensuring closure of the intestinal opening with precise suturing technique.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 Vicryl suture to close the full thickness mucosal layer of the anastomosis in a continuous running fashion.
The surgeon uses a 2-0 silk suture to imbricate the suture line, closing the second serosal layer of the anastomosis.
The surgeon briefly rechecks the perfusion of the anastomosis site, removes the sutures, and concludes the operation.
Thank you for your time.
